data.title: Alexandria Real Estate: Q3 Results Reinforce Bullish View
data.description:
I last looked at Alexandria Real Estate Equities, Inc. (NYSE:ARE) at the beginning of July. At that time, I thought the stock was an attractive investment opportunity because the valuation was well below what it typically traded with.

Even a subtle expansion in the multiple, coupled with the above-average dividend yield, could lead to double-digit total returns in my estimate.

Shares of Alexandria have gained only 1% since my article on the name was published, but my investment thesis remains very much intact. Let’s look at why I continue to believe that Alexandria is a strong buy today.

Takeaways from Alexandria Real Estate's Q3 Earnings Results

Alexandria reported third quarter earnings results on October 21st, 2024.

The company showed strength throughout its business. Revenue grew almost 11% to $791.6 million and was more than $11 million higher than expected. Funds from operations, or FFO, of $2.37 matched expectations, but were 1 cent higher on a sequential basis and compared favorably to $2.26 in the prior year.

Management provided updated guidance as well, with Alexandria expecting adjusted FFO in a range of $9.45 to $9.49, which is a tightening from prior guidance of $9.41 to $9.53. At the midpoint, this would be a 5.5% improvement from the prior year.

Growth on both the top and bottom lines and solid guidance has long been the story for the company. And Alexandria enjoys several trends that should continue to provide tailwinds to its business.

First, the company has a very high occupancy rate of 94.7%, which is a 10 basis point improvement sequentially and a 100 basis point increase year-over-year.

Part of this increase is thanks to the company’s focus on mega campuses. Mega campuses are those properties that are strategically located in clusters around major U.S. cities, such as Boston, New York City, San Diego, San Francisco, and Seattle, that bring together research, development, and testing.

These sites are critical to the healthcare sector as they bring together different biotechnology names for the purpose of finding new treatments for ailments. These sites are some of the most attractive places to work for those in the field, meaning that Alexandria’s properties will likely continue to be staffed.

This is vitally important as 76% of the company’s third quarter rental revenue comes from mega campuses, which is up from 74% in Q2 2024.

Alexandria’s leasing volume was especially high during the quarter. The company leased 1.5 million rentable square feet during the period. For context, the company had an average leasing volume of just 1 million square feet in the prior four quarters. Lease renewals grew 5.1%, showing that the company’s properties are very much in demand and clients are willing to pay up for them.

Alexandria also benefits from having more than half of its annual rental rates coming from investment-grade or publicly traded large tenants.

These factors go a long way in leading the company to a rental collection of 99.9% for the third quarter.

Alexandria should continue to see high rates of occupancy and rental collection, as the weighted average remaining lease term is 7.5 years for the portfolio. Looking at just the top 20 tenants, this figure expands to 9.5 years.

Another area I highlighted in my previous article was the strength of Alexandria’s balance sheet. Long-term debt did tick higher by 2.2% from Q2 2024, but the maturity schedule is spread out. Approximately a fifth of total debt matures in the next five years, including no maturities within the next year.

The weighted average remaining term is 12.6 years and a third of debt does not mature until after 2049, with an average interest rate of below 4%. Not only does the company’s debt maturity spread out over a very long period of time, but almost 98% of its debt is fixed rated, meaning Alexandria has significant visibility regarding how much its debt will cost to finance. Additionally, the company has liquidity of $5.4 billion.

In total, Alexandria has attractive properties located in key areas, collects nearly all its rent on time, maintains a fortress balance sheet, and has long-term contracts, most of which have annual rent escalators. In short, there is much to like about the company’s business model.

ARE Stock Valuation Analysis and Total Return Potential

Analysts are expecting the company to produce FFO near its guidance.

If so, then FFO growth would be nearly 6% from the prior year. This is lower than the five- and 10-year compound annual growth rates of 6.4% and 7.2%.

With the factors working in the company’s favor, I believe that 6% FFO growth, which is below the averages over the medium- and long-term, is sustainable.

Currently, Alexandria is trading at 12.5 times the expected FFO for the year. For much of the last decade, shares have mostly traded within a high-teens to mid-20s multiple. By this measure, shares are vastly underrated relative to its historical performance.

Part of the reason the valuation is much lower than usual is that higher interest rates have weighed on the REIT sector. The cost of issuing debt has been higher recently than it was during much of the previous decade.

Alexandria’s level of debt has expanded, but most of this debt was issued at fixed rates.

Furthermore, the Federal Reserve cut rates last month and the market seems to anticipate further cuts as well. While interest rates will likely never be as low as they were following the 2007 to 2009 financial crisis absent a major market issue, rates may have peaked and further cuts could occur as the economic data remains positive.

Lower rates would be good for the companies in the REIT sector and could provide additional expansion to the multiple, especially once the market starts to pay more attention to Alexandria’s fundamentals.

I reiterate my prior valuation range of 14 to 16 times FFO for the stock, which is still a discount to Alexandria’s long-term range, but incorporates a benefit from lower rates and a well-performing business.

Moving from the current valuation to this range would add a few additional percentage points to annual returns.

The stock’s dividend also deserves special mention. Shares are yielding 4.3% currently, which is not particularly impressive compared to the rest of the REIT sector. However, the current yield is more than 100 basis points higher than Alexandria’s 10-year average yield of 3.2%. Current shareholders are being paid to hold the stock at a much higher rate and at a much better valuation than it typically has over the last decade.

The dividend has a solid growth history as well, with both the medium- and long-term raises at just under 6% per year. And with an expected payout ratio of 55% for 2024, the dividend appears well covered. Future increases are also likely, which should help Alexandria’s 14-year dividend growth streak continue to grow.

My expectation for FFO growth of 6%, the dividend yield, and a benefit from an expanding multiple should combine for at least double-digit growth on an annual basis. And this is my base case. If the multiple reverts even closer to its long-term range, then total returns would be considerably higher.

Risks to Investment Thesis

The risks that I see for Alexandria have not changed much since the publishing of my first article on the name. Mega campuses continue to generate the bulk of rental revenue. That is a positive for the company as long as it maintains its very high occupancy rate. So far, this has not been an issue, and funding for the life science industry could be as much as $5 trillion.

Interest rates could remain elevated, making the cost of issuance more pronounced than in the prior decade. However, with Alexandria’s fixed rates and very long-dated repayment schedule in place, this is less of an issue for the company. If rates decline substantially, the company can refinance into a lower rate.

Finally, while the market has paid a very high multiple for the stock in past years, there is no guarantee that it will in the future. If the multiple settles around the current level, then total returns would be slightly less than I have forecasted here. Still, 6% FFO growth and the 4%+ dividend yield would still allow for a high rate of return even if the valuation were to be stagnant.

Final Thoughts

I have been very bullish on Alexandria since first looking deeply at the company in July. The most recent earnings report has worked to reinforce this rating, as Alexandria is a top name in the healthcare REIT space. It has several factors working in its favor, including a presence near important mega campuses, high occupancy and rental collection rates, a very reasonable valuation, and a higher-than-usual dividend yield.

If the market rerates the stock at a higher valuation to go along with 6% FFO growth and the 4%+ yield, Alexandria is likely to produce at least double-digit total returns. For these reasons, I continue to rate shares of Alexandria as a strong buy.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Boeing Loses $6 Billion
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Is all the bad news priced in now for Boeing? (0:15) Stocks slump on chip weakness. (2:35) Nvidia mea culpa. (3:58)

The following is an abridged transcript:

Our top story so far, is Boeing (NYSE:BA) at a bottom?

There’s a case to be made that all the bad news it priced into the plane-maker’s stock, with shares down slightly after Boeing reported a $6.7 billion quarterly loss.

In line with an earlier warning, Boeing reported a net loss of $9.97 a share on revenue of $17.8 billion. The company burned through $1.3 billion in cash, leaving it with about $10.5 billion at the end of September.

CEO Kelly Ortberg says the company has lots of work ahead before it can build a newly designed plane.

“We need to know what’s going on, not only with our products, but with our people,” he said in a memo to employees. “And most importantly, we need to prevent the festering of issues and work better together to identify, fix, and understand the root cause.”

He said the company must fix a broken culture, get smaller and improve execution on new plane models that have been delayed for years. Ortberg’s memo included remarks for his first earnings call.

Boeing in the past six years has worked to overcome two deadly crashes of its popular 737 Max jet, the pandemic’s effect on air travel and global supply chains, a criminal conviction, factory delays, a blowout of a door panel on an Alaska Airlines flight and a strike by 33,000 machinists.

SA Investing Group Leader Dhierin Bechai says: "A 1% decline in sales for the entire company is not extremely bad, but it should be noted that the biggest part of the impact on the work stoppage will likely be felt in Q4. The results were not pretty, but with the challenges Boeing faces, a $2 billion free cash flow burn, while a lot, is actually quite OK."

Also reporting this morning, AT&T (T) followed suit like its peer Verizon (VZ) and posted a small revenue miss and an adjusted profit beat for the third quarter while reaffirming its forecast for the full year.

But for the full year, it continues to expect adjusted EPS in the $2.15-$2.25 range (midpoint $2.20) vs $2.20 consensus, on wireless service revenue growth of 3%. Broadband revenue growth is seen up +7% and adjusted EBITDA growth is in the 3% range.

In today’s trading, Wall Street slumped on Wednesday, weighed down by a slide in technology and consumer discretionary stocks and a post-earnings plunge in some high-profile companies.

Meanwhile, a continued bond sell-off on bets that the Federal Reserve would not be as aggressive with rate cuts as previously expected also weighed on equities.

Technology was slammed as chip stocks fell, led by a big decrease in Arm (ARM). The British chip designer canceled a license deal that allows Qualcomm (QCOM) to use its intellectual property.

Among other active stocks, Texas Instruments (TXN) got some buying love after third-quarter beat as Wall Street analysts had largely positive views on the stock.

Benchmark reiterated its Buy rating and its $230 price target on the stock.

The analysts believe Texas Instruments elevated internal inventory should allow it to aggressively compete for sockets it was forced to pass on during the period of COVID-related supply constraints. That should continue the momentum it is currently enjoying in its non-auto and Industrial businesses. It also allows the company to flex its supply and capacity strengths as its broader core markets begin to see shipments return to consumption levels next year.

And Enphase Energy (ENPH) plunged after reporting a wide miss on Q3 adjusted earnings and a 31% Y/Y decline in revenues, while also guiding Q4 revenues well below consensus.

For Q4, Enphase issued downside guidance for revenues of $360 million to $400 million vs. $433.5 million analyst consensus, with non-GAAP gross margin seen in a range of 49%-52%.

In other news of note, Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang said the design flaw that caused production of its Blackwell line of GPUs to be delayed is now fixed and that it was entirely the semiconductor giant's fault.

"We had a design flaw in Blackwell," Huang said in Denmark, according to Reuters. "It was functional, but the design flaw caused the yield to be low. It was 100% Nvidia's fault."

Huang added that Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), which manufactures processors for Nvidia, helped the company recover from the yield issues and "resume the manufacturing of Blackwell at an incredible pace."

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, billionaire hedge fund manager Paul Tudor Jones says he is long gold (XAUUSD:CUR), bitcoin (BTC-USD) and commodities, and also noted that the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite index (COMP:IND) was a "great" inflation hedge.

On CNBC he said the "playbook" for the way to reduce U.S. debt to GDP ratio is to "inflate your way out."

He said: "You have a small tax on the consumer, and you run interest rates below inflation and nominal growth above inflation."

"All roads lead to inflation. I am long gold. I am long bitcoin. I think commodities are so ridiculously underowned, so I'm long commodities."

"I think most young people find their inflation hedges via the Nasdaq, that's also been great. I probably have some basket of gold, bitcoin, commodities and Nasdaq, something like that. And I own zero fixed income.”

data.title: Global Equities: Ratcheting Up Downside Protection
data.description:
By Bradley Krom

When equities are in a bull market, it is easy to forget about the pain of loss, but the risk of significant loss can be part and parcel of investing. Many investors do not have the risk tolerance to take on 100% equity risk. To offset that risk, investors have primarily used bonds to create balanced or moderate risk levels. However, simply diversifying only amongst stocks and bonds can fall short in some market cycles and fail to cover certain risks, as we experienced in 2022, when rising bond yields and falling equity prices made for a challenging year for almost every type of traditional asset.

Fortunately, there are options for further diversification. WisdomTree worked with Valmark1 back in 2021 to provide a new solution to help investors achieve long-run portfolio objectives while targeting an explicit floor on their investment losses. The WisdomTree Target Range Fund (GTR) is a unique tool that may be used to enhance a diversified portfolio, providing benefits not available through simple stock/bond diversification.

Target Range Refresh

GTR provides exposure to an allocation of U.S. large caps (SPY), U.S. small caps (IWM), developed market equities (EFA) and emerging market equities (EEM) in similar proportions to how many investors diversify globally. However, instead of simply owning equities outright, GTR buys one-year call options on each underlying ETF at the start of the year that are 15% in-the-money and sells one-year call options that are 15% out-of-the money - while the remainder of the Fund is allocated to a collateral account invested in fixed income instruments. Should markets decline, the Fund’s maximum annual loss or “floor” is the amount of capital invested in the call options - minus any proceeds received from selling options to offset the cost.

Assuming the options cost 15 cents to acquire (15% in the money options), 85 cents of every dollar invested in the Fund is in cash instruments. Of note, the roughly 85 cents invested in collateral, primarily Treasury bills, is now adding considerable value compared to when the ETF launched more than three years ago in a lower interest rate environment. While some investors may shy away from options strategies due to the lack of opportunity for dividends or interest, GTR currently has an SEC yield of more than 3%1, driven by the interest from the collateral investments. This helps complete the unique story of GTR providing upside participation, limited downside protection and income.

WisdomTree Target Range Fund (GTR)

Locking in Gains

When we first launched GTR back in 2021, a key question on investors’ minds was what happens to the Fund’s downside management if markets rally over the next year before the reset. In theory, an investor could purchase the Fund when markets are higher and expose themself to a higher beta strategy (with more significant downside risk) than an investor who owned it in January. To potentially offset this issue, we decided to create a mechanism whereby investors could lock in gains and ratchet up the “floor” of the strategy. Fortunately, after a strong start to the year, positions in U.S. large caps and small caps were both reset on August 1. On October 1, the options positions in EEM were also restruck to lock in gains.

Restrike in U.S. Large Caps

After a modest correction in April, positions in SPY rallied strongly as expectations for Fed rate cuts were brought forward. As a result, SPY closed the month of July above the strike price of 550. Also as a result, GTR rolled its options to lock in gains and bought calls at 470 and sold calls at 635.

ETF Price vs. Upper Strike Price (SPY)

Restrike in U.S. Small Caps

While large caps showed a steady rise, U.S. small caps rallied abruptly due to falling longer-term interest rates. Since small caps tend to be more interest rate-sensitive, the options were rolled into long calls at 191 and short calls at 260. With the benefit of hindsight, we know that taking chips off the table was a smart move, as rates have headed higher and small caps have fallen back below the original upper strike price of 220.

ETF Price vs. Upper Strike Price (IWM)

Restrike in Emerging Markets

Finally, in emerging markets, the big story was China announcing stimulus to help stabilize its economy and markets. While not as violent as the rally seen in small caps, Chinese equities pushed broader markets higher, triggering an option reset above 44. On October 1, GTR opened new long call positions at 39 and short calls at 53. While emerging markets remain above the original strike price, concerns about the lasting impact of China’s policy announcements have caused markets to retrace.

ETF Price vs. Upper Strike Price (EEM)

Conclusion

While GTR does not necessarily aim to capture 100% of equity market upside, we believe the flexibility built into the methodology to take chips off the table can have a meaningful impact on helping investors compound returns. While the path of markets remains uncertain, we believe strategies like GTR that have clearly defined downside management could add significant value to investor portfolios. We have witnessed advisors using GTR as a long-term strategic holding in a diversified portfolio, a risk management tool, a way to fund a specific goal and a way to get defined risk equity exposure with income.

1 Past performance is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted.

Important Risks Related to this Article

For current holdings of GTR, please click here. Holdings are subject to risk and change.

The Fund is actively managed and implements a strategy similar to the methodology of the TOPS® Global Equity Target Range™ Index (the “Index”), which seeks to track the performance of a cash-secured call spread option strategy. There can be no assurance that the Index or the Fund will achieve its respective investment objectives or that the Fund will successfully implement its investment strategy. Moreover, while the Fund seeks to target returns within a prescribed range, thereby minimizing downside investment loss, there can be no guarantee that an investor in the Fund will experience limited downside protection, particularly short-term investors, investors that seek to time the market and/or investors that invest over a period other than the annual period. The Fund’s options strategy will subject Fund returns to an upside limitation on returns attributable to the assets underlying the options. The Fund’s investments in options may be subject to volatile swings in price influenced by changes in the value of the underlying ETFs or other reference asset. The return on an options contract may not correlate with the return of its underlying reference asset. The Fund may utilize FLEX Options to carry out its investment strategy. FLEX Options may be less liquid than standard options, which may make it more difficult for the Fund to close out of its FLEX Options positions at desired times and prices. The Fund’s use of derivatives will give rise to leverage, and derivatives can be volatile and may be less liquid than other securities. As a result, the value of an investment in the Fund may change quickly and without warning, and you may lose money. Investment exposure to securities and instruments traded in non-U.S., developing or emerging markets can involve additional risks relating to political, economic or regulatory conditions not associated with investments in U.S. securities and more developed international markets. These and other factors can make investments in the Fund more volatile and potentially less liquid than other types of investments. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Bradley Krom, U.S. Head of Research

Bradley Krom joined WisdomTree as a member of the research team in December 2010. He is involved in creating and communicating WisdomTree’s thoughts on global markets, as well as analyzing existing and new fund strategies. Prior to joining WisdomTree, Bradley served as a senior trader on a proprietary trading desk at TransMarket Group. Bradley is a graduate of the Wharton School, University of Pennsylvania.

Original Post

data.title: Sangamo: Fabry Disease Gene Therapy Moves Forward With Positive FDA Regulatory Development
data.description:
Sangamo Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:SGMO) has been able to make several great advancements regarding its pipeline in the past year. The most notable program advancement would be regarding the fact that the company caught a huge surprise development from the FDA relating to its Fabry Disease treatment program. That is, it was noted by the U.S. agency that it would be able to file a Biologics License Application [BLA] of isaralgagene civaparvovec for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease [FD] under an Accelerated Pathway. The significance of this is that it brings about the ability to obtain FDA approval for this AAV gene therapy without the need to run a phase 3 study.

Even better, it has another positive worth mentioning like no need for it to run a post-approval phase 3 confirmatory study. What this new development does, despite having already been released as a milestone, is that it sets up two major catalysts in 2025. The first catalyst of which would be the release of intermediate eGFR slope data at 52-weeks from the phase 1/2 STAAR study in the 1st half of 2025. From there, if this endpoint goes well, then it would allow for the actual BLA filing of isaralgagene civaparvovec for the treatment of these Fabry Disease patients. This program alone is enough to generate value for shareholders, but it has another gene therapy in its pipeline that it is in the process of advancing with its partner Pfizer Inc. (PFE).

This would be regarding the use of giroctocogene fitelparvovec for the treatment of adults with moderately severe to severe Hemophilia A. It was reported that the phase 3 AFFINE trial met the primary endpoint of non-inferiority, along with superiority, of total Annualized Bleeding Rate [ABR]. Pfizer is expected to be in discussions with regulators to seek marketing approval of giroctocogene fitelparvovec for the treatment of these adults with Hemophilia A. An update on talks with regulatory agencies for it, along with several regulatory filings, are additional catalysts for investors to look forward to. With phase 1/2 STAAR study data using isaralgagene civaparvovec for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease, along with an Accelerated Approval pathway provided by the FDA, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

Surprise Pathway Is Likely To Result In Increased Shareholder Value

As I noted above, Sangamo Therapeutics received a really positive surprise from the FDA in terms of its Fabry Disease treatment program. That is, the FDA provided guidance that the phase 1/2 STAAR study, using isaralgagene civaparvovec for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease, would be enough for it to file a Biologics License Application [BLA] seeking Accelerated Approval for marketing in the United States. Before going over the released data from this phase 1/2 STAAR study, plus any catalysts to come out of this program, it is first important to go over what Fabry Disease is and what the possible market opportunity for it could be. Fabry Disease is a rare X-linked genetic lysosomal storage disorder whereby there is excessive fat [lipid] deposits on the tissues of the patient. The many parts of a person's body affected by this disorder are: Heart, kidney, skin and other tissues. One of the main problems with this disorder is the building up of fats on tissues, but why does this occur? It occurs because alpha-galactosidase A [alpha-Gal A] is deficient in the patient's body. Without this enzyme, the body is unable to break down globotriaosylceramide [Gb3], also known as GL-3. The point is that if this is not broken down, then it builds up on organs causing a significant amount of damage. Some symptoms that these patients with Fabry Disease experience are as follows:

  • Flu-like symptoms.
  • Gastrointestinal issues.
  • Hearing loss.
  • Proteinuria [excessive protein in the urine].
  • Swelling of specific parts of the body like ankle or feet.

The global Fabry Disease treatment market size is expected to be worth $3.34 billion by 2030. This is a very large market opportunity, especially when you consider that the FDA Accelerated Approval pathway just eliminated the need for another late-stage study, which could have taken at a minimum of 3 years to complete.

In order to see if Sangamo Therapeutics would be able to help treat these patients with Fabry Disease, it is in the process of running the ongoing phase 1/2 STAAR study. This particular mid-stage study recruited and dosed a total of 33 patients with a cut-off date of April 2024. With this cutoff date though, it was noted that 17 of 18 patients were off of enzyme replacement therapy [ERT], which is standard of care [SOC] for these patients. However, later on in September, the 18th patient was able to also forego the need to remain on ERT. This is good news in terms of helping these patients to forego the need to receive ERT, but what about the main focus endpoint necessary for Accelerated Approval. Well, in this regard, there is a very good start. Consider that of the 18 male and female patients, it was noted that all of them achieved a statistically significant improvement in mean and median eGFR levels. This ended up showing a positive annualized eGFR slope rate. There is one major item to point out though, which is that this is what was observed at 1-year follow-up only. Thus, there is no assurance that such a trend has been extended out to 52-weeks. Why this time frame? Well, that's because the intermediate efficacy endpoint to possibly receive Accelerated Approval of isaralgagene civaparvovec is eGFR slope at 52 weeks.

The announcement of the FDA giving Sangamo the ability to file a BLA of this gene therapy for the treatment of this patient population with an Accelerated Approval pathway, caused the stock price to trade higher by as much as 37%. If this is the case, then what on earth could possibly cause the stock price to trade higher on the back of this already released announcement? There are still plenty of catalysts on the way that could still boost shareholder value. The first of which would be the release of data from the phase 1/2 STAAR study, using isaralgagene civaparvovec for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease in the 1st half of 2025. Again, the point here is to see an intermediate time point of efficacy eGFR slope at 52 weeks, which will serve as an Accelerated Approval pathway endpoint. How can the allowance of such an endpoint of this happen? It also goes to a positive FDA Type-B interaction meeting, whereby it was noted by the U.S. agency that it would be okay to file a BLA based on this specific intermediate efficacy endpoint. If this efficacy endpoint is met, then the company would be in a position to file the BLA of this gene therapy for the treatment of this Fabry Disease patient population in the 2nd half of 2025.

Believe it or not, but there are possibly even two other catalysts that might arise from this one program alone, using isaralgagene civaparvovec for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease. The first catalyst of which that might occur would be the formation of a collaboration agreement with a big pharma partner. Why is that? The company has stated in its PR of the Fabry Disease data that it is in business development discussions with possible collaboration partners. Thus, the belief here is that it wants to establish a partnership with a pharmaceutical company that will help fund the commercialization of this gene therapy for Fabry Disease patients. The second catalyst of which would be in relation to talks established with the European Medicines Agency [EMA] that occurred back in June 2024. The purpose of this meeting was to talk about a proposed pathway to potential approval of this gene therapy in the European territories. If the company receives guidance that the phase 1/2 STAAR study is adequate enough for Accelerated Approval for the European territories as well, then this might end up being another surprise catalyst for investors to keep an eye on.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Sangamo Therapeutics had cash and cash equivalents of $27.8 million as of June 30th of 2024. The truth is that this is not a lot of cash and thus, it was expected that it would have to find a way to raise additional funds. Having said that, it entered into a global epigenetic regulation and capsid delivery license agreement with Roche Holding AG (OTCQX:RHHBY) subsidiary Genentech to develop medicines for neurodegenerative disorders. The agreement was made so that Genentech could use Sangamo's proprietary AAV capsid STAC-BBB to develop a tau protein target treatment along with another undisclosed neurology target. The promise of this STAC-BBB technology is its ability to have potent penetration of the blood-brain barrier, along with brain transduction.

This partnership deal that was established is expected to bring up a near-term milestone payment of $50 million, with the potential to also earn up to $1.9 billion in development and commercial milestones. Plus, the typical tiered royalties on net sales for any product that is ultimately marketed. The truth is to be cautious because it is highly likely that this company will raise additional cash. Why is that? That's because it believes that with its cash on hand, plus the $50 million in near-term milestone payments, will be enough to fund its operations into Q1 of 2025. The company burns roughly $36.2 million in cash per quarter. This is broken down into $24.2 million in R&D expenses and $12 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Sangamo Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be regarding the use of isaralgagene civaparvovec being explored in the ongoing phase 1/2 STAAR study targeting patients with Fabry Disease. The risk here is that even though 1-year follow-up data from this very same phase 1/2 study had a positive annualized eGFR slope rate, there is no assurance that this will still be the case at 52-weeks.

The second risk would be in terms of the advancement of giroctocogene fitelparvovec for the treatment of adults with moderately severe to severe Hemophilia A with its partner Pfizer. This big pharma is in talks with regulatory agencies across the globe to see if this gene therapy can eventually be approved for these patients. There is no guarantee that one or any regulatory agency across the globe is going to allow Pfizer and Sangamo to submit a marketing application. Even if a regulatory submission is made for this gene therapy targeting these adults with Hemophilia A, there is assurance that it will ultimately be approved for marketing.

The third risk would be in terms of the epigenetic global regulation and Capsid delivery license agreement made with Genentech to advance novel medicines to treat neurodegenerative disorders. Sangamo is likely going to receive the $50 million in near-term milestone payment for sure, but the risk here is that the $1.9 billion in developmental and commercial milestone payments are going to depend upon clinical success. In addition, there is no guarantee that the tau protein or other STAC-BBB AAV capsid to be developed will end up achieving positive data. Having said that, it is quite possible that Genentech could end up terminating the partnership program entirely.

The fourth and final risk to consider would be with respect to the financial position that this company is in. That's because as I noted above in the "Financials" subsection, it believes that it only has enough cash on hand to fund its operations into Q1 of 2025. The risk here is that it is highly likely that it is going to need to raise additional cash soon enough. Especially, when one of the next possible catalysts causes the stock price to trade considerably higher.

Conclusion

Sangamo Therapeutics has done well to advance a few of the clinical products from its pipeline. I believe that most notable achievement was the fact that it received a huge positive surprise from the FDA, in that it would be allowed to file a BLA for Accelerated Approval of isaralgagene civaparvovec for the treatment of patients with Fabry Disease. Investors will not be left in the dark because there are still several catalysts on the way with this program alone. However, the most crucial milestone for this program being the release of eGFR slope 52-week data from the phase 1/2 STAAR study.

That's because without positive data from this particular trial, then the other expected milestones are not likely to happen. The other gene therapy in its pipeline is another program that investors should keep an eye on. That's because giroctocogene fitelparvovec has already been able to achieve success in the phase 3 AFFINE trial, targeting patients with moderately severe to severe Hemophilia A patients. Pfizer is expected to be in talks with regulatory agencies and if positive, then this would result in a huge positive surprise to the upside as well. It was able to obtain $50 million in cash from Genentech for the development of STAC-BBB AAV capsids to target neurodegenerative disorders.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: McDonald's: Brace For A Drop, Buy Lower After Outbreak
data.description:
Introduction

McDonald’s Corporation (NYSE:MCD) was hit with the worst kind of news a restaurant chain can face: A major interstate E. coli outbreak has infected at least dozens and likely many more people and killed at least one person. First things first, people are seriously ill, there has been at least one death, and more people may die from this or require hospitalization. It's important to recognize the human aspect of this story before getting into the investing angle.

That said, we are here to talk stocks and make money, and MCD will almost surely sell off substantially from where it is today. The question is how much and when it becomes a buy for brave investors looking for long-term outperformance.

Investors need to understand the big picture regarding something like this. This has happened before and, in times past, has been a great buying opportunity. The first thing I thought of when hearing this news was what happened to Chipotle Mexican Grill, Inc. (CMG) in 2015 and 2016. The same situation might play out for MCD. It’s also important that investors understand the initial selloff may just be the beginning of MCD’s share price decline. The severity of illnesses, how widespread the outbreak is, and who and how MCD is to blame, are all key factors that will impact the stock price. How the company handles the crisis will also matter.

For anyone tempted to jump in immediately, consider this: Major news events that cause a sudden drop or spike in a stock price are often just the beginning of a trend for some weeks or months. The breaking news is often followed by further declines or increases in stock prices as investors absorb key information, consider liability and damages, and revise earnings estimates. For this reason, it’s unlikely anyone needs to rush into buying the stock.

Parallels With Chipotle

We can look to the CMG case to find parallels and differences in each story as the MCD outbreak unfolds. It’s too early to understand the whole picture, but we can probably find something to help inform us what might happen to MCD.

Chipotle’s food safety crisis, in 2015, unfolded over a period of months and involved several separate incidents of different illnesses. At first, there was a norovirus outbreak in California in July 2015. Then, in August 2015, a salmonella outbreak occurred in Minnesota. Matters got far worse in October and November 2015, as an E. coli outbreak started in Washington and Oregon and spread to 11 states in total. That December, there was another norovirus outbreak at a Boston area Chipotle, and by February 2016, all CMG restaurants were closed for safety precautions and company-wide safety improvement measures. Some 500 people were sickened at Chipotle restaurants, and the company’s reputation was severely damaged. Its stock price cratered around 60% before gradually staging a comeback.

It may be too early to tell, but if McDonald’s can limit this to this one outbreak, and it doesn’t end up being extremely large, this may work out much differently for McDonald’s than it did for Chipotle. Chipotle was a much younger company with a less certain future at the time, and the reputational harm was likely much greater than what MCD would face. I would also argue that MCD has a moat through its brand, economies of scale, and low-cost production, while at the time CMG had its crisis, it likely did not have a strong moat.

Chipotle stock fell roughly 60% from before the crisis, and I don’t think we should expect McDonald’s to fall 60% from earlier today. However, we can lay out some prices where the stock might be a great long-term buy so that we’re ready if and when the stock gets to those prices.

I doubt that McDonald’s won’t return to being a stalwart company in the long term, and I doubt that the long-term financial picture for the company will change based on what happened this week. But it might be safe to model slower growth for the next year.

Valuation

MCD has grown EPS at a CAGR of 7.8% since 2018. Its dividend per share has grown at a CAGR of 8.4% during this time. MCD’s average trailing P/E has been 27.55x since 2018.

I’m using a 3-scenario EPS exit multiple model to value the company.

Even if we assume that MCD’s EPS growth rate slows somewhat, it appears that MCD will be trading in a reasonable range following its after-hours drop.

In my base case, I factored growth rates of 6.0% for years 1-3, 7.0% for years 4-8, and 6.5% for years 9-10. The estimated exit multiple used is 22x earnings, a discount from its historical average.

In my bear case, I factored projected growth rates of 5.5% for years 1-3, 6.0% for years 4-8, and 5.5%, and 5.0% for years 9 and 10. The estimated exit multiple used is 18x earnings, a significant discount to its average P/E.

In my bull case, I factored projected growth rates of 7.7% for years 1-10, which is slightly lower than MCD’s CAGR since 2018. The estimated exit multiple used is 25x earnings, roughly in line with its current multiple.

The discount (hurdle) rate used is 9.0%, representing the annualized share price return if the model comes out as a 0.0% discount to fair value.

Based on these inputs and the after-hours price of MCD as of 10/22/2024, the stock could be fairly valued for a 9.0% price appreciation plus its current dividend yield, which is roughly 2.5%. The EPS calculations can be seen below.

Risks

The full damage to MCD’s reputation and financials will not be clear for some time. It is possible this will be quite substantial. In a worst-case scenario, it could take years to recover. I caution all investors to be patient or purchase with a large margin of safety.

My model is not based on in-depth knowledge of MCD’s cash flows and should be treated as an illustration of potential stock returns and not a definitive projection of cash flows or earnings.

Conclusion

MCD stock will fall based on this news, I believe it could become a buy soon, but want to either wait for more information on the severity of this outbreak or buy with a significant margin of safety. My model shows the potential for a roughly 11.5% annualized total return, given my inputs.

MCD is likely to trade lower for some time, and my plan of action is to watch closely and potentially open a position if the price falls roughly 5% below $296, which would be roughly $281 per share. However, I might wait for an even lower price, depending on how the market reacts and what kind of news comes out. A share price below $250 may make it easy to buy the stock.

Either way, I plan to do more thorough research in the coming days before making any purchases.

For now, I consider MCD a hold, but this may change to a buy quickly.

data.title: Gold, Silver Print Fresh Highs As The DXY Eyes Key Level
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Gold prices have smashed through the 2750 handle despite the US Dollar continuing its advance. The precious metal has attracted bids, with some believing this could be down to markets preparing for a potential Trump Presidency in the US.

There does not appear to be a single factor driving gold prices, but rather a combination of rising uncertainties for global market participants to consider. Global conflicts, trade wars and a potential Trump Presidency are at the forefront and that is leaving out the uncertain economic situation in many countries.

Despite sky-high stock prices in the US and what seems to be improving sentiment, Gold, a notorious safe haven bet, reveals a different story. Gold prices have been on a tear this year and with many people still viewing the precious metal as a safe haven, are market participants telling us something?

Silver (XAG/USD) Demand/Supply Dynamics Bodes Well for Bulls

A commodity that has flown under the radar of late has been silver, as prices soared to new all-time highs. Silver is trading in the mid-34’s at present, with market analysts noting that more gains could be in store for silver prices.

Looking more closely at silver, the appeal is quite evident. Silver is used in a host of electronic products manufactured today, and the rise of tech is only going to increase the demand for silver.

Given the physical shortage already present in silver markets, there is a huge possibility that the rally could continue. Looking at the chart below, you can see the huge discrepancy between demand (blue line) and supply (white line) which I expect will underpin prices moving forward.

US Dollar Index (DXY) Eyes a Rise to 105.00 Key Level

The US Dollar continues its rise on positive data and fading rate cut expectations. Markets have been digesting comments from Fed Policymakers over the last week or so, which have largely leaned on the hawkish side.

As markets adapt to fewer anticipated rate cuts in the US compared to more aggressive cuts by other central banks, the US Dollar may be set to keep rising. There is growing talk of a ‘Trump trade’ as US Treasury yields have experienced some interesting swings off late. This may be another area to focus on when looking at the greenback moving toward the US election.

US Dollar Index Daily Chart, October 23, 2024

Technical Analysis Gold (XAU/USD)

From a technical analysis standpoint, Gold continues to print fresh highs and languish in overbought territory. As we know, markets are able to languish in overbought territories for extended periods of time and thus the Gold rally could continue.

Gold is currently flirting with the key level at 2750 with a drop lower opening up a retest of the support area at 2739. Lower down, we could look at areas of support around 2724 and 2714.

The upside provides us with less insight, with today’s highs at 2758 an area to keep an eye on before the psychological level at 2775.

Gold (XAU/USD) Four-Hour (H4) Chart, October 23, 2024

Support

  • 2739
  • 2724
  • 2714

Resistance

  • 2758
  • 2775
  • 2800

Original Post

data.title: Canadian National Railway: Downgrade To Hold On Near-Term Uncertainty
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Canadian National Railway Company (TSX:CNR:CA) previously (25th April) with a buy rating as I thought the drop in valuation was an attractive entry point, especially with the weekly data (at that point of time) pointing to positive volume growth. I downgrade my rating from buy to hold as I am wary about the potential miss in FY24 EPS guidance given that the weekly available data so far is not supportive. I still see the medium-term upside as attractive if CNR can achieve its three-year adj EPS CAGR guide, but I want to see more evidence that it can do so before I change back to a buy rating.

3Q24 earnings (announced on 22nd October)

CNR 3Q24 results were a mixed bag. Total revenue grew 3.1% y/y to CAD4.11 billion, beating the consensus estimate by 80 bps. However, EBIT fell by 10 bps, missing consensus expectation by 90 bps, mainly driven by the higher operating ratio [OR], which stepped up by ~120 bps y/y to 63.1%. Consequently, recurring net income fell moderately from CAD1.108 billion to CAD1.085 billion. But adj EPS grew by 1.8% y/y to CAD1.72 as management bought back 3.8% of shares outstanding vs. 3Q23. Despite the mixed performance, management reiterated their FY24 and medium-target guidance, which calls for low-single-digit percentage EPS growth for FY24 (note this is an downgrade from the mid-to-high-single-digits EPS growth target prior and double-digit percentage growth at the start of this year), and three-year (FY23-FY26) EPS growth of high-single-digits CAGR through 2026 (this is also a downgrade from the 10% to 15% target prior).

Given the performance so far and the outlook for FY24, I am less excited about the bull case for CNR in the near term. Perhaps the most concerning aspect I have is that CNR may not meet its FY24 EPS growth guidance. Volume performance in 3Q24 was not great as it flipped negative (after turning positive in 2Q24), primarily due to the port diversion, labor situation (Teamsters Canadian Port Strikes), and wildfires in the rail’s network. The lingering impact from these events is still hurting CNR as the quarter-to-date carload data continues to point to a 3% y/y decline with the most recent week’s carload growth rate of -6% y/y.

For CNR to meet management FY24 guidance of low-single-digits adj EPS growth, 4Q24 adj EPS need to grow ~8% (YTD average adj EPS growth is ~0.2%, and assuming 2% FY24 adj EPS growth, this implies high-single-digits EPS growth for 4Q24), which means like-for-like growth (before share buyback) needs to be ~4% (CNR bought back 4% share so far this year). Assuming the same level of OR as 3Q24 for 4Q24, this implies volume needs to surge to ~4% in 4Q24 and that the remaining 10 weeks left of the quarter need to see very strong growth (as the quarter-to-date trend is down ~3%).

From what I heard during the call, it seems that the key driver to volume growth will stem from international intermodal growth as CNR improves its US mix. This is not impossible, but intermodal is just ~40% of total car load, and the quarter-to-date growth is just ~3%. For intermodal to move the needle, volume growth has to surge sharply from here, which is tough to forecast with certainty given that peak season arrived early this year and there is still lingering labor uncertainty at the Canadian port.

Operational excellence deserves highlight

What was impressive and deserves a highlight is the network health and fluidity, which are evident from the improved key metrics. In particular, freight car velocity increased to 219 miles per day in September and through dwell improved to 6.8 hours after June's forest fires and August's TCRC work stoppage. With freight car velocities of 225 miles per day and through dwells of 6.5 hours, both of these metrics have been steadily improving in 4Q24, according to the weekly operating metric. The reason I highlight this is because it indicates that CNR has the operational capacity to handle any incremental volume growth (supposedly if intermodal volume grows as management expected).

Valuation

I am downgrading my rating from buy to hold mainly due to the near-term uncertainty—CNR may not meet its FY24 guidance, which may cause the share price to fall further. From a valuation perspective, while CNR used to trade at a 7% premium, I believe the market is unlikely to attach a premium given the potential miss in earnings (the stock now trades at 19.3x forward PE, which is in line with where peers are trading). Relative to peers (CSX, Union Pacific, Canadian Pacific Kansas, and Norfolk Southern), the CNR revenue growth outlook is rather mediocre, in line with the average at mid-single-digits. Hence, there is even lesser reason to believe a premium is warranted (ie no valuation rerating benefit).

That said, I wouldn’t brush this stock aside and ignore it. If CNR is able to convince the market that volume growth is back online and track well against its three-year adj EPS CAGR guidance, the upside may be attractive even without multiples moving upwards. In my model above, assuming CNR buys back 3% share/year, this implies that like-for-like adj EPS growth needs to be in the high-single-to-low-teens digit range for FY25/26 (equates to ~9% adj EPS growth CAGR). This is a high bar to get over when FY24 adj EPS growth is guided for 2% (with potential to miss). As such, despite the upside, I am not willing to commit to a buy rating for now until I see more evidence that CNR can achieve this mid-term guidance.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for CNR as I am afraid CNR may miss its FY24 EPS guidance, leading to further pressure on the share price. While it has demonstrated operational improvements, the weakness in volume growth is concerning. I do think that CNR medium-term upside is attractive (if it can achieve the guidance), but I believe it is prudent to wait for further evidence of its ability to deliver on its growth targets before considering a more bullish stance.

data.title: Baker Hughes Q3 Results: Decent Performance, But I Am Cautious For Now
data.description:
Introduction

Baker Hughes Company (NASDAQ:BKR) just reported Q3 results that were a mixed bag, in my opinion, but the markets seem to love the robust backlog and continual improvement in margins. I wanted to take a look at the numbers in more detail and give some comments on the outlook and what, I think, is the biggest catalyst in the future. With somewhat conservative estimates, the company seems to be trading at fair value. With some uncertainty regarding the main catalyst, I am going to remain on the sidelines for now until we get more clarity on what is going to happen next year.

By the numbers

Starting from the top, BKR's revenues came in at $6.9B, up 4% y/y and -3% sequentially. Sales missed consensus estimates by $300m. Non-GAAP EPS came in at $0.67, a beat of 6 cents, while GAAP EPS came in at $0.77.

In terms of efficiency and profitability, the company's gross margins came in at 22.3%, operating margins came in at 13.4%, and net margins came in at 11%. Compared to the same quarter last year, margins stood at 20%, 10.7%, and 7.8%, so we can see a decent improvement y/y, which led to the company's Adjusted EBITDA improvement of 23% y/y.

Remaining performance obligations, or RPO, remained essentially flat at $33.4B, down $100m. Orders, however, came in at $6.7B, down 22% y/y and down 11% sequentially.

Industrial & Energy Technology carried the company's top line, while Oilfield Services & Equipment came in flat y/y.

In terms of the company's financial position, as of Q3, the company had around $2.66B in cash and equivalents against around $6B in long-term debt. That's not a bad position to be in, in my view. The company's operating income easily covers the interest expense on debt almost ten times, so the debt outstanding is not an issue. It would be wise to start paying some of it off instead of acquiring more, but I don't think it should be a priority.

On the back of these results, the company is confident in achieving its full-year EBITDA guidance midpoint, which is around $4.3B.

So, looking at how the market is taking in the news, BKR's share price is up around 4% as of writing. The market seems to like what the company has delivered this quarter.

In my view, this was quite a mixed bag of performance. The low top-line growth is offset by a decent improvement in efficiency and profitability y/y and a somewhat stable and impressive RPO.

Comments on the Outlook

My biggest gripe is the company's orders are much lower than last year, which suggests a slowdown. However, the management doesn't seem to think so and says that "Orders remain at solid levels." At $6.7B, they are solid, but a decline of 22% y/y seems to be downplayed and not addressed at all in the remarks provided by the management. However, in the earnings call, the management was quite excited about the future of orders and expected them to continue to grow.

In terms of growth opportunities, I've been quite bullish on LNG growth, and it is good to see that the management agrees with this sentiment. The company expects to see LNG demand growth to be up 75% over the next 17 years, with natural gas also still contributing to the demand, while oil remains flat.

New LNG projects are slated to grow quite slowly in '24, but the following year, the U.S. Energy Information Administration forecasts that there was going to be around 18% y/y growth. It will have grown by 40% in '28, representing the higher capacity growth in the global LNG industry, which mirrors what the management expects the sector to do. It also looks like the management may be a little conservative in their growth estimates.

Energy transition and carbon reduction are playing a massive role in LNG's growth. LNG is considered a cleaner-burning fossil fuel compared to coal, which will help reduce greenhouse gas emissions over the long run. The shift from coal is happening in many countries to meet climate goals. In my view and many others, LNG is a bridge for the transition to fully renewable energy sources that no doubt will become the future of energy.

As energy demand continues to increase due to the rising population, increased transportation use, and energy requirements, especially to support the rapidly growing AI and data center demand, LNG may have another use, which could be very synergistic. I'm talking about LNG cooling power-hungry AI data centers. I've come across an article that shows how LNG can benefit the further growth of AI data centers. The more LNG plants we need in the future, the more potential to put data centers there to use LNG's residual cold reuse.

However, this is still only in theory because air-cooling is still viable, with direct liquid cooling the natural next step as power consumption continues to creep up. However, if at some point DLC is not sufficient anymore, the next step could well be LNG cooling. There is a reason why it is not available yet. It is too costly and could be very risky.

In terms of the company's operation performance, the management has done a commendable job at improving efficiency and profitability in the past five years, which looks to continue for years to come. We can see that the company has beaten its margin expectations back in Q1 '24 and is now guiding for a much higher margin profile than just six months ago. It looks like the company will reach its EBITDA margin target of 20% much earlier than anticipated. During the earnings call, the management said that this EBITDA margin target is not the end goal, which means we should expect even more efficiency eventually.

The company mentioned that they are still working on removing duplication and right-sizing capacity in some segments. It seems to be one of the company's main priorities right now, so I am very confident in the company's margin profile improvement over the next couple of years. The management has been adamant that the 20% EBITDA margin is not the end goal but rather a point along the way. That gives me a lot of confidence that the company is heading in the right direction, and I like this shift in strategy. Let's look at some valuation assumptions.

Valuation

I like to look at conservative estimates when it comes to valuing a company, rather than being too optimistic in the outlook. This way, I am getting a built-in margin of safety, and the model becomes a stress test for what the company is capable of achieving.

For revenues, I went with around 3% CAGR over the next decade, which I think ignores the potential that the energy transition has going forward, especially the aforementioned LNG project growth. These assumptions should be easy to beat for a company like BKR.

For margins, the management has been working tirelessly to improve efficiency and profitability, so I wanted to see how well it would perform if its margins continued to improve further. I decided to improve the company's gross margins by around 500bps over the next decade. I think this is still on the lower end of possible margin expansion given that the company's gross margins expanded by over 200bps y/y, so another 5% expansion in a decade is achievable.

For the DCF model, I went with the company's WACC of around 8.7% as my discount rate and 2.5% as my terminal growth rate. Additionally, I decided to discount the final intrinsic value by 10% to give myself even more room for error in these estimates. With that said, the company's intrinsic value is around $35 a share, which means the company is trading at its fair value, in my opinion.

Closing Comments

Given the conservative assumptions above, the company appears to be quite enticing in my opinion and earns a spot on my watch list. However, I am not too keen on starting a position right now because I would like to see what the next quarter is going to bring and what the management will have to say about the future order progression. It doesn't seem like it was a big deal that orders came in 22% lower than last year; however, I would like to see how that item is going to progress in the remainder of the year.

The biggest catalyst remains the LNG sector, but I am quite cautious of this sector in the short run, especially during this election time. As we all know, President Joe Biden decided to pause key LNG export permits earlier this year, but VP Kamala Harris has been a bit less strict on this issue and backs diverse energy investments, and will not ban fracking. Nevertheless, it's all up in the air, and I would rather keep an eye out while remaining on the fence for this one.

data.title: Dividends On Track: Why I'm Bullish On Canadian National's Long-Term Potential
data.description:
All financial numbers in this article are in Canadian dollars unless noted otherwise.

Introduction

It's time to discuss the Canadian National Railway Company (NYSE:CNI), Canada's largest Class I railroad and a company I have often covered in in-depth articles and included in multi-ticker research.

My most recent coverage was published on July 24, titled “Off The Rails? Not Quite. Buying Canadian National On Weakness.”

Unfortunately, since then, New York-listed shares have returned -3%, lagging the 8% return of the S&P 500 (SP500) by a substantial margin. As we can see below, the railroad has been unable to keep up with the S&P 500 since 2022, causing an increasing divergence from the market's total return.

This applies to most cyclical companies. The leading ISM Manufacturing Index entered contraction territory back then, which has predicted weakness in the manufacturing sector and caused investors to look for gains in other areas, including fast-growing FANG+ stocks that dominate the S&P 500.

Personally, I own three North American Class I railroads. All of them have performed poorly since 2022. In fact, most transportation stocks have not done well.

The chart below compares the distance the Transportation ETF (XTN) trades below its all-time high (in %) to the ISM Manufacturing Index. As we can see, transportation stocks stood no chance the moment sentiment went south.

However, as most of my readers will know, I have written plenty of bullish articles on railroads recently, as I have been busy buying my favorite stocks at attractive prices. After all, we want to buy high-quality cyclical stocks when times are bad. That way, we enjoy these investments once economic growth improves.

Unfortunately, it sounds easier than it is, as the current macroeconomic environment is very complex. We're dealing with cyclical headwinds, secular tailwinds from economic re-shoring, and labor issues like major strikes in Canada.

In this article, I'll elaborate on all of this, as I explain why Canadian National remains one of my favorite dividend growth plays going forward.

So, as we have a lot to discuss, let's get to it!

Strength Despite Cyclical Headwinds

One of the best things going into 3Q24 earnings was the fact that the company adjusted its guidance on September 10. This lowered expectations tremendously.

The company obviously knew that labor strikes and wildfires would hurt its business. By alerting investors on time, it prevented creating an unforeseen surprise.

This is what Reuters reported one day after the guidance announcement:

CN said the work stoppages, as well as the wildfires in Alberta, led to a quarter-to-date impact of about 20 cents per share to its earnings.

However, its operations have now recovered following several months of uncertainty, the company said.

The company now expects adjusted diluted earnings per share (EPS) growth in the low single-digit percentage range, compared to its previous forecast of a "mid to high single-digit" percentage growth. - Reuters.

In light of that adjustment, the company did an impressive job managing its operations after implementing its scheduled operating plan roughly 30 months ago. It allowed the company to rapidly bounce back after the wildfires in Northern Alberta in July and the recent labor-related shutdown.

On top of handling record Western Canadian grain volumes, it has maintained strong service levels. As we can see below, 3Q24 car velocity, terminal dwell, and local service levels are all equal or better compared to the prior-year quarter. In general, the Canadian players are known for their operating resilience. The CN railroad has proven this again.

Nonetheless, the company used the 3Q24 earnings call to explain it is dealing with a softer macroeconomic outlook than initially expected, especially in the company's merchandise business, construction, and automotive franchise.

Because the railroad's network originates more than 80% of traffic, with access to all economic hotspots in Canada, the U.S. Midwest, and the corridor down to New Orleans, the company is very prone to cyclical headwinds.

This network also provides wide-moat benefits, as the company is a critical supply chain player with a network that is impossible to re-create for new entrants.

Regarding cyclical headwinds, looking at the handy overview below, we see this translated to 2% fewer carloads. However, on a revenue-ton-mile (volumes adjusted for distance) basis, volumes were up 2%. This was supported by long-haul international intermodal and refined petroleum products, two of the areas that are doing well, in general.

Moreover, strong grain demand led to a 4% increase in grain and fertilizer RTMs. When including inflation, pricing power provided 1% higher revenue per RTM and 4% higher revenues per carload.

Overall, the company's revenues were up 3%.

The bad news is that work stoppage and wildfires made it impossible for the company to grow its margins. As a result, the operating ratio increased by 110 basis points to 63.1%, which is still a good number, although it kept the 3Q24 operating income flat.

Buybacks saved the day for earnings per share, which rose by 2%.

Canadian National Railway Shareholders Remain In A Great Spot

For the fourth quarter, the company expects intermodal traffic to remain strong, supported by international momentum and a recovery in West Coast volumes. However, labor uncertainty could still impact Canadian operations.

In the cyclical automotive sector, volumes are expected to bottom. Grain is expected to remain strong. I expect that to last, as harvests this year should be good on both sides of the U.S./Canadian border.

Moreover, petroleum and chemicals are expected to perform consistently. Unfortunately, other sectors are expected to see more headwinds, in line with cyclical headwinds.

As part of these quarterly expectations, the company reaffirmed its September guidance update, expecting low-single-digit EPS growth and a healthy balance sheet with a 2.5x leverage ratio. To maintain this leverage ratio, the company has paused its buybacks.

Beyond 2024, the company is upbeat about secular growth opportunities, as it has a promising pipeline expected to drive half of its future volume growth.

[...] we've built up a strong pipeline of opportunities with our customers that are unique to this network. We see this driving more than half of the volume growth in coming years. So we're confident that these will allow us to grow in spite of some of the macro challenges. - CNI 3Q24 Earnings Call.

Over the past ten years, the company has bought back more than a fifth of its shares. This includes a pause of almost two years during the pandemic, which was a smart move, as it allowed the company to protect its balance sheet when some of its peers risked higher leverage ratios. This is rewarded with an A- credit rating from S&P Global.

The dividend was hiked by 7% on January 24. Since then, the company has paid $0.845 per share per quarter. This translates to a yield of 2.2%, protected by a 41% payout ratio using 2025E EPS of $8.28.

The five-year compounded annual growth rate of the dividend is 10%. Moreover, the dividend has been hiked every single year since 1995, making it one of the most consistent dividend growth stocks in the industrial sector.

In general, the mix of very reliable (and safe) operations, conservative balance sheet management, and dividend growth consistency have made it a very trusted dividend growth stock. I expect that to remain the case, potentially supported by more aggressive buybacks down the road.

The only reason why I do not own CNI is my investment in its Canadian peer, Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP). I also own Union Pacific (UNP), which has a similar dividend growth profile.

CNI Stock Valuation

Using the FactSet data in the chart below, analysts expect 1% EPS growth this year. This makes sense in light of challenges and company guidance. 2025 and 2026, however, are expected to see 13% and 11% growth, respectively.

After recent stock price weakness, Toronto-listed shares trade at a blended P/E ratio of 21.1x, slightly below the 10-year average of 21.2x.

Using a 21.2x multiple, the company has a fair price target of $196, 26% above the current price. Including its 2.2% dividend, we get a total return outlook of 13-14%. This also applies to New York-listed stocks, except CAD/USD fluctuations can influence the total return.

Needless to say, cyclical growth is a wild card. If we get a growth bottom over the coming months, we could see substantially higher returns in the years ahead.

Takeaway

Despite recent cyclical headwinds, Canadian National Railway remains a compelling dividend growth stock.

The company's ability to maintain operational resilience through labor strikes and wildfires, combined with solid revenue growth and strategic long-term measures, supports its outlook.

With a promising pipeline of opportunities and a well-managed balance sheet, CNI is in a great spot to deal with macroeconomic challenges.

While I don't own the stock due to my positions in Canadian Pacific Kansas City and Union Pacific, I see CNI as a solid pick for dividend investors looking for consistent growth.

Pros & Cons

Pros:

  • Operational resilience: Canadian National has proven its ability to bounce back from disruptions like labor strikes and wildfires, maintaining strong service levels.
  • Dividend growth: With a 7% hike earlier this year and a 10% five-year CAGR, it's one of the most consistent dividend growth stocks in the industrial sector.
  • Strategic network: CNI's wide moat, including its crucial supply chain role and irreplaceable network, sets it apart.
  • Secular tailwinds: Long-term growth opportunities, especially from international intermodal and grain demand, remain strong.

Cons:

  • Cyclical exposure: The company is vulnerable to macroeconomic headwinds. Due to its size, cyclical headwinds immediately pressure revenue growth.
  • Operating Environment Risks: Factors like fluctuating fuel costs and labor expenses pose risks to the company's operating margins and profitability.
  • Buyback pause: As long as buybacks are paused, EPS growth lacks one driver.
  • Currency Changes: Because Canadian National is a Canadian company, foreign investors need to be aware of the impact of currency fluctuations on the value of their investments and dividends.

data.title: After ASML, Could AMD Be Next? (Q3 Preview)
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) is set to report Q3 earnings on October 29th and this is going to be a very important release.

Just last week, ASML Holding (ASML) released its earnings and investors were not pleased. The stock tanked over 10% and dragged the semiconductor market with it.

With AMD reporting soon, we may get the answer to many of the pressing questions the ASML debacle has raised.

  • Is the AI mania over?
  • Is ASML an exception, or the canary in the mine?
  • Will AMD's results help turn the stock around?

In my opinion, AMD is one of the few companies that can realistically take market share from NVIDIA (NVDA).

Though ASML is also in the semiconductor industry, it plays a very different role from AMD, and their poor guidance may not actually be reflective of the broader demand for chips.

In my last article on AMD, I highlighted the stock was cheap for an AI company, and that continues to be the case today as the price has not moved much. The doubt cast by ASML gives us a chance to pick up more stock at a discount.

What happened to ASML?

It's worth having a quick look at the ASML earnings, which the market punished so brutally. There were three factors coming together that catalyzed the sell-off.

  • Revenues below expectations

  • Poor guidance

  • Export controls

Net bookings were very weak, down 52% QoQ. While ASML's earnings have come up in the last year, there's certainly been a lot of volatility, and growth has not been explosive like with NVIDIA.

Meanwhile, guidance was lowered, which probably ties into the new restrictions by the Dutch government.

The Dutch government will be requiring chip companies to apply for permits in order to export their products, and China is ASML's largest client.

Could AMD face the same fate?

But that's old news now. The next big chip company to report will be AMD. Could we see a big sell-off in this stock too?

Before we get into the earnings preview, it's important to highlight the similarities and differences between AMD and ASML.

AMD, like NVIDIA, is a chip designer. They are essentially coming up with the technological designs that are used to build semiconductors.

On the other end, we have Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), which is a fab company that assembles the chips.

Somewhere in the middle, there's ASML, which sells the equipment necessary for the manufacturing of said chips, focusing on lithography.

This is to say that not all chip companies are created equal. While ASML is an important company in the space, its particular challenges, revenues and margins are quite different from chip designers like AMD and NVIDIA.

Specifically, I would argue that while we have seen ASML do well in the last year, its revenue growth hasn't been equal to that of other companies in the sector. We can't simply extrapolate from the company's earnings what the whole semi-industry will look like.

We must dive into the specifics of each company, which we will do below with AMD.

Q3 Earnings Preview

AMD will report earnings next week. Here's what we can expect.

  • EPS of $0.91, up 30% YoY

  • Revenues of $6.71 billion, up 15.72% YoY

  • Non-GAAP gross margin of around 53.5%, 53% in the prior quarter.

Overall, AMD is still expected to show healthy growth in earnings.

As we can see, the company has narrowly beat estimates in the last four quarters, and investors will be expecting nothing less.

It's interesting to note that earnings are expected to climb significantly too in Q4, so guidance will be a very important factor in these earnings.

We can also see that there have been a lot more downward revisions in EPS over the last three months than up revisions. Interestingly, it seems to be the opposite for revenues.

Perhaps the strong monthly numbers that TSM has been posting have increased analysts' expectations, but clearly there's some idea that margins may not do as well as once expected.

My 2 Cents

The way I see it, a lot of the bad news seems to have been priced in here for AMD, and there's room for a nice surprise at earnings.

First off, there are strong differences between ASML and AMD. The former is much more dependent on foreign clients, while AMD's revenues are more evenly distributed geographically.

On the other hand, AMD has enjoyed strong growth in earnings, and if we look at valuations, it's actually well-priced.

Particularly if we look at forward PEG, AMD is the cheapest out of its peer group. The key here is for AMD to deliver on these lofty earnings expectations. So far, the company hasn't done anything to make me doubt them.

Finally, if we look at the price chart, technical analysis shows this could be a decent place to buy.

After rallying to over $220, AMD has come down significantly. It actually almost tapped the 61.8% retracement back in August. Since then, we have been grinding up, and now we are finding support at the 200 EMA.

The way I see it, this is a key level to hold, but we also have strong support at $147 and then $130 as shown by the two trend lines.

Below that, we could revisit $120, but this would be such an attractive price I don't think we will get there.

Takeaway

All in all, I think AMD is still a good long-term hold. The ASML earnings have not changed my view. AMD is still cheaply valued and has good growth prospects. Earnings moves are very hard to predict, but I think we have a good chance of heading higher next week.
